original_reasoning: Arabic slack coarse physicality matches the Greek form best at the conceptual level of looseness rather than surface definition.
The filled-out body sense supports receive or be filled more than directly.
Kaha - boast/speak loudly vs 'to speak loud, boast, brag' - direct semantic match

الشمظ=يشمظ الإنسان بكلام / تحريك دون العنف (one speaks gently with words / movement without violence). λίσσομαι=to beseech, entreat, pray. Both describe soft, non-violent verbal acts directed at another person seeking favor. Strong overlap in the gentle-persuasion domain.
نستر=place name; Νέστορα=Nestor (accusative). Possible Greek proper name loan
The second element strongly recalls camel, but as a compound place name the semantic preservation is less direct than Gamala.
Arabic good bodily form and Greek full-grown or adult share a clear field of mature physical development.
Arabic describes a coarse thick-formed but slack old person; the Greek compound plausibly shares the core of looseness or relaxation.
